title: Humidity, steam and showers
summary: Moist air can reduce dryness and feel soothing, but steam has not been shown to cure a cold or reliably shorten symptoms. Cool-mist humidifiers may help comfort when air is dry, provided they are kept clean and indoor humidity stays controlled.
evidenceSnapshot: Cochrane evidence does not establish meaningful benefit from heated humidified air. Humidifier guidance focuses more on comfort and safe maintenance than antiviral effect.

interventions.name: Cool-mist humidifier in dry air
interventions.finding: May ease dry nose or throat and make congestion feel less irritating.
interventions.practical: Keep indoor relative humidity about 30–50%; empty, dry and clean the unit as directed.
interventions.cautions: Dirty units can aerosolise mould or bacteria; excess humidity promotes mould and dust mites.

interventions.name: Warm shower
interventions.finding: May temporarily loosen secretions and feel relaxing.
interventions.practical: Use normal shower temperatures and a non-slip bathroom.
interventions.mechanism: Warm humid air and muscle relaxation.
interventions.cautions: Does not need to be extremely hot.

interventions.name: Bowl-of-boiling-water steam inhalation
interventions.finding: No reliable added benefit and a real scald risk, especially for children.
interventions.practical: Choose a warm shower or controlled humidifier instead.

interventions.name: Essential oils in humidifiers
interventions.finding: No good evidence that they treat viral infection, and many devices are not designed for oils.
interventions.practical: Do not add unless the manufacturer explicitly permits it.
interventions.mechanism: Odour may alter perceived airflow without opening the airway.
interventions.cautions: Can irritate asthma, poison children/pets or damage the device.

practicalSteps: Measure humidity rather than guessing if using a humidifier.
Use distilled or demineralised water when the manufacturer recommends it.
Clean on schedule and never let stagnant water sit for days.
If the home already has dampness or mould, do not add humidity.
avoidOrCare: People with asthma or allergies should stop if symptoms worsen.
Steam comfort should never replace asthma treatment or assessment for breathing difficulty.

myths.claim: Steam kills the virus in your airway.
myths.reality: Temperatures that would reliably inactivate viruses would injure the airway; clinical trials have not shown a cure.
High humidity encourages mould, bacteria and dust mites and can worsen respiratory symptoms.

redFlags: Worsening wheeze or breathlessness around a humidifier
Any steam burn, especially in a child
Mould growth or persistent condensation in the room
unsettled: Whether specific subgroups with very dry indoor air gain meaningful symptom relief remains uncertain.
